output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 1.0, "R1.4": 0.4, "R1.5": 0.3, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.2}}

Summary: The section's framing dramatically overstates what the reported numbers show; the central problem is a mismatch between the headline claims ("substantial gains... across every evaluation setting," "universally applicable," "consistent WER reductions across read, conversational, and far-field conditions") and the actual table of results, four of six of which are neutral or negative for ClarifyASR.

Key issues:

1. The evidence contradicts the headline claim. Of six conditions, only two (LibriSpeech test-clean, TED-LIUM-3) show improvement. LibriSpeech test-other (+0.1), CHiME-6 (+0.5), and VA-Log (+0.3) are *worse* than the baseline; Switchboard is a tie. The abstract/opening paragraph and the closing paragraph both assert universal/consistent gains. This is not defensible from the data presented. The claims must be rewritten to say the method helps on clean read speech and degrades or fails to help in noisier/conversational/far-field conditions.

2. Asymmetric treatment of noise. The text dismisses the three regressions as "within the noise band of a single training run" or attributable to "clarification-trigger noise," but offers no such caveat for the two wins. If a single training run cannot distinguish a 0.3–0.5 point regression from noise, it also cannot establish a 0.2-point (test-clean) or 0.5-point (TED-LIUM-3) improvement as real. Either report multiple seeds with variance, or apply the same epistemic standard to both directions.

3. "Headline result" framing is editorializing, not analysis. Calling test-clean and TED-LIUM-3 the headline while burying the other four conditions is exactly the move a careful reviewer will flag. The two "wins" are also the two cleanest read-speech conditions — that is itself the finding, and it should be stated as a scope limitation rather than hidden.

4. The recommendation in the final paragraph ("recommend it as a default component for production deployments") is unsupported and arguably contradicted by the VA-Log result, since VA-Log is the only condition that actually resembles a production voice-assistant workload, and ClarifyASR is worse there.

5. The user-study claim is overreached. n=12 with a 9/3 split is suggestive at best; a one-sided exact binomial test gives p ≈ 0.07. Reporting it as evidence that "perceived-quality gains generalize beyond the WER metric" is too strong — especially since the WER gains themselves do not generalize. Report the n, the split, and (if you want) a confidence interval, and characterize it as preliminary.

6. Missing methodological detail needed to interpret results. The "accepted/rejected clarifications" scoring rule materially affects WER and deserves more than one sentence: how is acceptance determined (user action? oracle? heuristic?), and is the same rule applied to the baseline? If the baseline cannot clarify, the scoring is asymmetric by construction and the 0.2-point test-clean delta may be a scoring artifact rather than a recognition improvement.

7. Missing the cost side of the ledger. A 1.3-turn median dialogue overhead is a substantial UX cost that the framing ignores entirely. Even on the conditions where WER improves, the paper needs to argue that the WER reduction is worth the added turns; right now the cost is mentioned and then dropped.

8. The 4.7% trigger rate is reported without conditioning on dataset. Trigger rate almost certainly varies sharply between test-clean and CHiME-6; an average across heterogeneous conditions hides the mechanism. Break this out per condition, and correlate trigger rate with WER delta — that is likely where the actual story lives.

9. Novelty/positioning is absent. The section never situates selective clarification against prior interactive-ASR or confidence-gated rescoring work, so a reader cannot tell what is new. At minimum, the introduction needs one or two sentences distinguishing this from prior clarification-question and confidence-thresholded approaches.

10. Prose-level issues. "Across every evaluation setting we considered" is directly false given the table. "Establishing... as a universally applicable strategy" is a single-paper overreach regardless of results. "Drive the headline result" reads as spin. Recommend rewriting the opening and closing paragraphs to claim only what the data supports: improvements on clean read speech, neutral-to-negative results on conversational and far-field speech, and a preliminary user-preference signal.

Suggested reframing for the contribution: "ClarifyASR improves WER on clean read-speech benchmarks (test-clean, TED-LIUM-3) but does not improve, and may slightly degrade, performance on conversational (Switchboard), far-field (CHiME-6), and in-the-wild assistant (VA-Log) conditions. A small pilot suggests users may prefer the clarifying behavior even when WER is unchanged, motivating further study of when clarification helps." This is a publishable, honest version of the same work.

Uncertainty: The binomial p-value (~0.07) is computed from the stated 9/12; I have not verified the specific scoring convention for "accepted clarifications" against any standard reference, and recommendations about prior-work positioning assume the literature on interactive/clarifying ASR is non-empty (which is true to my knowledge but I did not search it here).
